segments.text: Nicknames for the president's board.
David Morgan, it was called the House of David, and some of the others.
Let's see, Gilchrist?
Not for him, later.
Harrington?
Uncle Tom's cabin.
Oh, okay.
Very good, very good.
Rudder?
The General's shack, I wonder, or something like that?
Describe how the students' relations with a faculty member, what was their manner, their style, if they came to your office?
Were they respectful?
Oh yes, always respectful, always, yes sir.
Even Murray Trimble.
Fine football player?
Yes.
With what?
Lost a hand?
Lost a hand.
He got in big trouble.
During those years, particularly the 50s and 60s, describe for me a typical work week for you.
Normally you left here and got to the office about what time?
Well, usually I made quite a number of hours.
Some conferences pretty much took up my day.
So you, did you walk over or drive?
I drove.
So, more often than you'd think, actually.
So you'd leave here at about what time?
About 4.
The students, and your work day, you, so you were, as far as you recall, by the late 50s, what was a normal teaching load?
When did it go down from 18 hours?
About 15 hours.
About 15?
About 15.
And about how many of those were introductory courses and how many advanced?
Do you remember?
Well, at a senior level...
No, about how many of them were junior, senior compared to the introductory freshmen?
We had a great many more freshmen, really, but we had more freshmen sections because everyone was...
In the English department, what were some of the popular elective courses?
And then, other than that, I think probably American Lit Survey.
What were your favorite sections to teach?
Well, I was always interested in the American, taught a novel score, but we taught across the...
So, you would leave here, leave the house...
Well, I went between 7 and 30.
I drove that short.
Where'd you park?
In the academic building.
And there was no fee.
Did you come home for lunch?
Yes.
And then you went back, and normally your day on the campus would end at about what time?
And five, six day a week, because it was a week.
Six day a week, because it was Tuesday, Thursday, Saturday.
What did you and your family, what, the social life, as the girls got a little older, what did you all do in the community?
Well, of course, they were wrapped up in school activities, and we did all the PTA stuff.
And of course, we went to all the, any concert or play or anything.
Where were the concerts and the plays held on campus?
Guyon Hall.
And make believe that I had never been in Guyon Hall.
Describe it for me.
Well, it was sort of a Greek revival, sort of a lovely old building.
And it seated 1,200 men?
About 1,200 men.
It had religious emphasis.
Do you remember some famous people that came to speak or perform?
Because I've heard some of the students talk about the big bands that came and performed in...
Sabisa for their dances.
Who were the, in the 50s, who were the key administrators?
Who was the Dean of Arts and Sciences during those years?
I knew that was coming.
Was Dr. Abbott in there?
I suspect so.
And then Frank Hubert comes in 59.
Yeah.
All right, let's talk about him.
Describe him as the Dean.
He was very effective.
Took some ribbing here and there about having been an high school band director and so on.
But he was very approachable.
The, Mr. Rutter comes in January of 59.
Were there any faculty comments about his selection, his appointment, as you recall?
What about David Morgan?
How did the faculty see him?
I think there was some satisfaction, an outsider sort of.
I got here at about the same time Mr. Rutter did.
And at first, I wasn't impressed with Mr. Rutter's academic style and ability.
But I later came to realize that he had many other abilities.
Let's see.
Describe College Station in the 50s and 60s as a very separate town.
Well, it of course was still very small.
And if you need anything, just get in.
Get what they want.
If we locked the house.
And people, I presume, have always parked over here for football games.
Have you ever had any incidents, problems on a football weekend of somebody that parked in front
of the house and bothered anything?
No, Captain.
Never?
They're really regulating that now, but never many times.
Back to campus.
Who were some of the key individuals that you, other than maybe the President and so forth,
some other key individuals that you think helped make A&M in the 50s, 60s, 70s?
Advisors, counselors, administrators?
Wayne Stark, let's see.
Yeah, Wayne Stark.
What role did he play that made the thing better?
Really, he was tremendous.
He involved so many students and taught them so much and involved them in the activities.
Made all kinds of really good efforts.
Trying to give you some of that sophistication and memories.
As Bill said, they don't have tremendous influence and made all the difference in the world
when they were students.
Did you ever go on any of those?
Yes.
Can you describe one of those?
Well, I remember going to Luke and C. I believe an opera, maybe at Jones Hall with
a group of students.
And we met with John...
Lindsey?
Lindsey, yeah.
John Lindsey thereafter, and he took us to a club, which is a little farther.
What was the effect on the students of that weekend?
Well, I think they were a little bit awestruck.
But I think they did it.
I think that for...
We got to go on one of those.
And I got the impression that this was almost like helping those students to raise their
sights.
That you could become able to enjoy this kind of art and culture and so forth.
Yeah, I think he had that in mind.
Pinky Downs.
Yeah, I met Pinky almost as soon as I arrived.
I think I was in the Campus Post.
I met Pinky Downs, and I think he always gave his...
Before the MSC was built, what was the center for extracurricular activities?
Certainly back when we came here, when the girls came down from Texas University.
There were a few activities at the old YMCA, I understand.
Yes, there were some.
Was Mr. Cashin still active?
Yes, yes.
I know nothing about him.
Please tell about him.
He was a much loved person.
Where did a student in the 50s and 60s, where did a student go for advice, counsel?
Probably some faculty health center, of course.
I don't really remember that we had any.
See, I have the belief that in that era, faculty members thought that one of their unwritten
responsibilities was to be a counselor in addition to being a teacher.
That teacher... Was he teaching?
Extended far beyond the classroom.
Yeah, I think so.
I've been proud of A&M on that score.
I think that attitude, we tried to make contact with students.
Actually, I think we're still trying, although it's pretty tough.
All right.
We were talking about presidents.
Jack Williams, what was your impression of him and his role as president here?
I was very favorably impressed with him.
He was one I knew before I came here.
He was a great person.
He was a great man.
He was a great man.
He was a great man.
He was a great man.
He was a great man.
He was a great man.
He was a great man.
He was a great man.
He was a great man.
He was a great man.
He was a great man.
He was a great man in my opinion, and had a tough time .
What do you think were some of your...
40 years?
How long I taught?
Yeah, I taught for 32 years.
Well, my slot was 46 to 78.
Oh, okay.
I've had 21 years since then from which I grew up.
That's about it.
That's about it.
get right during those years if there were people that you could pull out and
thank them and Pat them who were those people that made a difference for this
school
do any of the president's do you have any favorites among them what are some
of the key decisions you think that were made during that were very important to
A&M today well we integrated for one thing I remember very well we conducted the summer school
enrollment speech of all and the word was passed along to us there will be black students enrolled in the role and it was a piece of a thing
that somebody made that decision and of course eventually
do you remember any even a hint of an incident over integration
I don't think so
I don't either
yeah that was much easier
I now know that Mr. Rutter was a key person in getting those students here
that summer because he called the Pentagon and had them send as they
normally would send army personnel to finish a degree and I now know that he
called and said send some including some black students no academic danger no
conduct problem and then when the fall began
since everything had gone well in the summer someone couldn't organize a group
say it won't work it had worked do you do you remember as women began do you
remember any any kinds of any incidents or awkward moments or pranks he was a regular
he was a regular
yeah yeah
I remember he talked about perhaps starting maybe in American literature also we dug into
it and we had to have a biography of each professor who would teach a regular course
we fought all that out in the 1950s
and were there any snags as you remember or did it pretty well go for it
well it pretty well went along
and when did the doctoral program were you still a faculty member when the doctoral program began
yes any key people that deserve a pat on the back there
well certainly this department had worked like a tiger in Washington and Lee Martin
ok very
was Maxwell the dean by this time David Maxwell
no we had this one
oh ok so it would still have been Hubert I guess then
huh ok was English one of the pioneer humanities social sciences graduate area
let's see do you remember anything that students ever did to go out of their way to be polite or show appreciation for anything
did any student or parent of the student ever attempt to intimidate you unless my student my son passes this or that
did anybody ever attempt to in any way bribe you with anything that they did to you
or bribe you with anything
no I only had one parent I had any idea where he was
well I didn't
I got I remember who I got in touch with but I got in touch with some authority
I said we have about 3 days later they discovered him hiding out in a dome
there was something wrong with the young man
in age well past that he was scared to death
his father named Steele
He got in touch later and I eventually let him leave.
He came back and took the question.
Do you recall any, what you now in hindsight think of, as administrative interference with the way the academic program was operated in English?
In terms of, was there a merit salary system and the promotions were handled, as far as you were concerned, with reasonable fairness and equity?
Who are some special students that you remember now with pride and joy?
Well, I've always been, I've kept up with six or four attorneys.
Oh, did you?
Describe him as a student.
Well, Jack had been on campus, but he didn't throw any weight around.
And he'd gone to a small high school, because he was a, yes.
Jack made it be, as I recall, he did something notable.
Yeah.
Was he a good student?
Let's go back to Part D for a second.
Did, under Bear Bryant, was there ever any, did anybody ever try to influence you in grading an athlete?
Not directly, but he had a, he had a…
Jerry Claiborne?
Jerry Claiborne.
Kentucky?
Yeah.
Anyway, Jerry would come…
Did any other coach ever, in any sport, ever attempt to…
attempt to ask for any special favors?
Did anyone in the Corps?
And you didn't hear any about any board member's son or any general's son?
No, I didn't.
I guess I was lucky.
Other special students?
You mentioned Stick Stengel and Jack Pardee.
Others that you remember with pride?
A young lady named Ann Abbey.
She was Sam Melton's daughter.
Mm-hmm.
Ann Abbey, Texas Ironman.
Oh, good.
I remember her.
She did extremely well.
Did you become involved with any extracurricular student groups?
Oh, yes.
I remember students.
Oh, yeah, I did a lot of that.
Of course, Duke and I was on the…
I worked with Skona for a year, and I was on the MSC Council, which I remember.
I remember Chad Edwards was one of the best representatives.
From Waco and Temple?
Yes, I remember Chad very well.
Oh, I remember John Rogers.
He was a pagan in the insurance company in Dallas.
Bud Whitney actually started Skona.
And then there was John Samuels, the New York millionaire.
He was a sponsor of the opera and all that sort of thing.
He was one of my debaters.
Yeah, I remember those folks well.
If you could go back…
This is a very broad question.
Very broad question.
If you could go back and make any changes in decisions or procedures, what would you change, being blessed with hindsight?
I was asking you to offer advice, based on your experience, anything you would have changed then and or advice to give to leaders now about A&M.
Well, I would have tried to change then.
Yeah.
It's just what I think ought to be added something in the…
During those years that you were active on the campus, who were the key people whom you thought were most sensitive to this need to broaden and to deepen?
I guess all English faculty felt that way.
What about key campus leaders?
Well…
